evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 5/10 — Voice oscillates between a believable GP persona and generic SEO listicle boilerplate.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — A few sharp opinions (ignoring WHO, Ramadan killing resolutions) sit beside many hedged platitudes.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — '45°C car park' and 'January self had no idea Ramadan was coming' are vivid; most other phrasing is stock.
Specific citations (names, places, prices, dates) 5/10 — Mentions DHA, MOHAP, Ramadan, 1.5-2L water, but no prices, clinic names, specific dates, or people.
Point of view vs neutral survey 3/10 — Presents consensus guidance without taking a clear position on what actually works.
Quotable sentence 1/10 — Nothing memorable or shareable; all sentences are interchangeable wellness filler.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with duplicated H1, a bureaucratic TL;DR, and a meta description before the human hook appears.

intro_hook 4/10 — The opening sentence is pure throat-clearing: it restates the title verbatim and adds no tension, surprise, or specificity that earns continued reading. There is no hook, no stakes, no counterintuitive claim. The reader has no reason to feel this post is for them rather than a generic wellness article.

specificity 6/10 — Several bullet points and transition sentences float in abstractions ('small, useful adjustments', 'longer-lasting health benefits', 'sustainable progress'). The one genuinely specific data point — 'A typical DHA-aligned panel at Valeo runs AED 650 and covers HbA1c, LDL, vitamin D, and TSH — expect results in 48 hours' — is excellent and shows what the rest of the post lacks. The 'Key takeaways' and 'Long-term behaviours' sections in particular rely on vague language where named numbers, clinic names, or cited figures would anchor the claims.
